content.problemStatement:
An array BBB of length NNN (N≥2)(N \ge 2)(N≥2) is said to be good if the following conditions hold:


For all 1≤i≤N1 \leq i \leq N1≤i≤N, 2≤Bi≤1062 \leq B_i \leq 10^62≤Bi​≤106
gcd(Bi−1,Bi)≠1gcd(B_{i-1},B_i)\neq1gcd(Bi−1​,Bi​)=1 for all iii  (2≤i≤N)(2 \leq i \leq N)(2≤i≤N).
You have an array AAA of length NNN (2≤Ai≤1052 \leq A_i \leq 10^52≤Ai​≤105). You want to make the array AAA good.

To do so, you can change **atmost** ⌈2⋅N3⌉\left \lceil{\frac {2⋅N} 3}\right \rceil ⌈32⋅N​⌉ elements of AAA.

Print the final array after changing AAA to a good array. If there are multiple possible final arrays, **print any of them**.

It is guaranteed that AAA can be made good  after changing **atmost** ⌈2⋅N3⌉\left \lceil{\frac {2⋅N} 3}\right \rceil ⌈32⋅N​⌉ elements of AAA.


Input Format

The first line of input contains a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases. The description of TTT test cases follow.
The first line of each test case contains an integer NNN - the length of the array.
The second line of each test case contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,...,ANA_1,A_2,...,A_NA1​,A2​,...,AN​ representing the initial array AAA.


Output Format
For each test case, output a single line containing NNN space-separated integers, denoting the elements of the final array after converting AAA to a good array. The iii-th of these NNN integers is iii-th element in the final array.


If multiple arrays exist which satisfy the conditions, print any of them.


**Note:** Final array should differ from original array at **atmost** ⌈2⋅N3⌉\left \lceil{\frac {2⋅N} 3}\right \rceil ⌈32⋅N​⌉indices.
constraints: Sum of N over all test cases ≤ 2·10^5
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case, the first line contains an integer N (2 ≤ N ≤ 10^5). The second line contains N space‑separated integers A1, A2, …, AN (2 ≤ Ai ≤ 10^5).
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line with N space‑separated integers B1, B2, …, BN – the final good array. The array must differ from the original array in at most ⌈2·N/3⌉ positions. Any valid array may be printed.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Test case 1: N=3. Keep A1=6, replace A2 and A3 by 6. Changed positions =2 ≤ ⌈2·3/3⌉=2. All adjacent gcds are 6.\nTest case 2: All elements already equal, no changes needed.

solution.approach: We process the array in blocks of three consecutive positions. In each block we keep the first element unchanged and replace the next two elements (if they exist) by the same value as the first one. Let the block start at index i (0‑based). We set B[i] = A[i]; if i+1 < N then B[i+1] = A[i]; if i+2 < N then B[i+2] = A[i]. Because every Ai ≥ 2, all assigned values stay within the allowed range. For any adjacent pair inside a block we have both numbers equal to A[i], therefore gcd = A[i] ≥ 2 ≠ 1. Between two consecutive blocks the last element of the previous block equals the first element of that block, so the gcd condition also holds across block boundaries. In each block we change at most two positions, hence the total number of changed positions is ≤ 2·⌊N/3⌋ + (N mod 3) ≤ ⌈2·N/3⌉, satisfying the limit. The algorithm runs in O(N) time per test case and uses O(1) extra memory besides the output array.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, O(ΣN) over all test cases.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) for the output array (can be done in‑place, still O(N)).
